Comment: Standard fixed-wing on a RealFlight plane model (conventional RealFlight fixed-wing channel order: rf0=aileron, rf1=elevator, rf2=throttle, rf3=rudder). The "px4" values are HIL_ACTUATOR_CONTROLS.controls[] indices and MUST match PWM_MAIN_FUNC* in ROMFS/px4fmu_common/init.d-posix/airframes/1200_flightaxis_plane (and HIL_ACT_FUNC* in init.d/airframes/1200_flightaxis_plane.hil): controls[0]=aileron left, [1]=elevator, [2]=throttle motor [0,1], [3]=rudder, [4]=aileron right (surfaces are [-1,1]). DESIGN RULE: the "rf" and "px4" columns are deliberately IDENTICAL - RealFlight channel N == PX4 output channel N - because the airframe params are chosen so that controls[] already comes out in the RealFlight model's own channel order. The ordering decision lives in PWM_MAIN_FUNC*/HIL_ACT_FUNC*, exactly as ArduPilot puts it in SERVOn_FUNCTION and PX4's own gz bridge puts it in SIM_GZ_*_FUNC*. If you change those params, restore this file to identity by fixing the params - do not re-scramble this table.
AileronComment: LEAVING rf4 (RF ch5) UNMAPPED IS DELIBERATE AND COSTS NOTHING. The airframe declares split ailerons (CA_SV_CS0=left -> controls[0] = rf0, CA_SV_CS1=right -> controls[4] = rf4), but the two allocator outputs are EXACT MIRRORS: measured in SITL with an armed fixed-wing roll correction, actuator_servos.control was [-0.61392, +0.61392, ...] and stayed an exact negation at every sample. CS1 therefore carries no information CS0 does not, and - this is the part that is easy to get wrong - CS0 already swings the FULL +-1 range, not half of it. PX4's pseudo-inverse of the 1x2 roll row [-0.5, +0.5] is [-1; +1], so the arithmetic and the measurement agree: mapping the left aileron alone drives a single mixed RealFlight aileron channel at full authority, with no loss of roll. This also matches the reference implementation everyone's RF models are built against: ArduPlane defaults SERVO1_FUNCTION to k_aileron - ONE combined aileron on ch1 (ardupilot/ArduPlane/Parameters.cpp:1414-1417, CH_1 aileron / CH_2 elevator / CH_3 throttle / CH_4 rudder), and SIM_FlightAxis.cpp passes servos 1-12 through untouched, so an ArduPilot-driven RF plane gets nothing on ch5 either. RF ch5 consequently reads UnmappedDefault 0.5 (verified: constant 0.5 across every frame of a SITL run). ONLY add a row if your RF model genuinely drives the two ailerons on separate channels - and note that mapping it wrongly is worse than not mapping it, because RF models commonly put flaps, spoilers or gear on ch5 and a full-amplitude roll-correlated signal would drive those instead. If yours really is split, add {"rf": 4, "px4": 4, "scale": "bipolar", "reverse": <see below>} and choose "reverse" by watching the aircraft: RealFlight models differ in whether the second aileron channel is reversed inside the model's own servo setup, so both conventions exist. Correct is the value that makes the two surfaces move in OPPOSITE directions; if they both deflect the same way you have the roll cancelled out and a pitch/drag upset instead, so flip "reverse".
RfModel: informational only - aircraft is selected inside RealFlight
